title: Erdős #349 assembly piece: α ≤ 1 fails to be a good pair
informal_statement: One of the four assembly pieces for the integer_isGoodPair_iff characterization used toward Erdos Problem #349: if alpha <= 1 (with t, alpha > 0), the pair (t, alpha) is NOT good -- it is false that eventually every integer k is a finite sum of distinct elements of the range of n |-> floor(t*alpha^n). This is a sublemma supporting the integer_isGoodPair_iff characterization, not a resolution of #349 itself (which asks, in general, for which real t, alpha the sequence floor(t*alpha^n) is additively complete -- still genuinely OPEN).
informal_proof_idea: Since alpha <= 1, every term t*alpha^n is bounded within [0, t], so the whole range floor(t*alpha^n) is bounded within [0, floor(t)]. Any finite subset sum is then bounded above by the fixed constant C = sum_{i=0}^{floor(t)} i, but eventually-true would require arbitrarily large k (in particular k > C) to be representable -- contradiction.
formal_statement_pp: theorem erdos_349_alpha_le_one_not_is_good_pair : ∀ (t : ℝ), 0 < t → ∀ (α : ℝ), 0 < α → α ≤ 1 → ¬ (∀ᶠ k in Filter.atTop, k ∈ {n : ℤ | ∃ B : Finset ℤ, ↑B ⊆ Set.range (fun n : ℕ ↦ ⌊t * α ^ n⌋) ∧ n = ∑ i ∈ B, i})
